metrics.narrative: YTL articulated qualitative operational pain but has not quantified a business case in ROI or cost-savings terms across the call history. On the 1 April 2026 demo call, Tan Chaa Yehh (SAP Manager, Decision Maker) described YTL's current state: 'We are currently using SAP MM module for procurement, but it is very basic. We need a more robust e-procurement solution to manage our supplier base, streamline requisitions, and improve compliance across our multi-plant operations.' Tan also noted on the 21 April 2026 workshop debrief call that YTL's existing SAP MM process lacks workflow automation, supplier collaboration tools, and audit trails, leading to 'manual workarounds, delayed approvals, and compliance gaps'. However, neither Tan nor any other stakeholder has stated a quantified target: no mention of hours saved, error-rate reduction, cost-per-PO, or procurement cycle-time improvement. The pain is confirmed and operational (manual process, compliance risk, multi-plant complexity), but the quantified value case required to justify a $250k investment to the Economic Buyer (CFO or C-level, not yet engaged) is missing. Without a co-built ROI model, Zycus cannot anchor the senior-management approval conversation in financial terms, and YTL's apples-to-apples pricing comparison may default to a cost-minimization lens (cheapest vendor wins) rather than a value-maximization lens (best TCO/ROI wins). Next move: co-author a quantified ROI framework with Tan this week that estimates hours saved (requisition-to-PO cycle time reduction), error reduction (manual rework eliminated), and compliance risk mitigation, using Zycus benchmarks from comparable manufacturing/cement customers, so Tan can present a defensible business case to senior management alongside the vendor comparison.

champion.narrative: Tan Chaa Yehh (SAP Manager, Decision Maker per OpportunityContactRole) is the developing champion, but as of the 21 April 2026 call his engagement is neutral and evaluative, not overtly advocating for Zycus over SAP Ariba or the third RFP participant. Tan has been the primary YTL contact across the deal: he attended the 1 April demo, led the 29 April workshop, and provided the 21 April debrief. He has decision-making authority over the technical RFP evaluation and will present the vendor comparison to senior management for final approval (21 April call: 'I will score the vendors and present the comparison to senior management'). Tan has access to the Economic Buyer (he is the conduit to senior management), so structurally he is well-positioned to champion Zycus if he becomes convinced. However, evidence of active advocacy is limited: on the 21 April call, Tan's tone was neutral and methodical ('We are evaluating three vendors. I need to see the pricing breakdown and compare apples-to-apples. If Zycus is 2x or 3x more expensive, that will be a problem.'). He has not pushed back on competitors in Zycus's favor, has not shared internal docs (e.g., the RFP draft, YTL's scoring rubric, competitor quotes), and has not driven urgency (the RFP promised for May, then 19 June, is now delayed to early July with no pushback from Tan on the delay). The Next_Step entry dated 1 June says 'building strong champion', but this appears to be George John's aspiration, not a confirmed state—no call transcript or activity log shows Tan acting as a strong champion (e.g., advocating for Zycus in internal meetings, coaching George on how to win the RFP, or blocking competitors). Tan's engagement is active but cooling: last verified buyer touch was 13 June (Salesforce LastActivityDate), and the 2 July Next_Step update reports that George 'connected with the relevant stakeholder on 2nd July' (rep-initiated outreach, awaiting buyer reply per the engagement pulse). If Tan remains a neutral evaluator and a competitor (especially Ariba, which has SAP-native integration and may be perceived as lower-risk for the S/4 migration) offers better pricing or a stronger fit on a YTL must-have, Tan could swing to that vendor. Without a strong internal advocate pushing Zycus's differentiation (SAP-maintenance integration, practical AI, two-envelope evaluation) to senior management, Zycus could lose the RFP scoring or the senior-management approval even with good technical fit. Next move: convert Tan from neutral evaluator to active champion by co-authoring the senior-management vendor-comparison presentation with him—offer a draft slide deck that frames the apples-to-apples comparison with Zycus's differentiation baked in (TCO not just license cost, SAP-maintenance integration, compliance/two-envelope strength, S/4 readiness), request a 30-minute working session to refine it together, and position it as 'helping Tan make the strongest possible business case to senior management'. Co-creation builds co-ownership and locks Tan's endorsement before he presents to senior leadership. Also: ask Tan directly, 'What would make you confident recommending Zycus over the other two vendors?' to surface any unstated concerns and address them proactively.

competition.narrative: YTL is running a three-vendor RFP process: Zycus, SAP Ariba, and one unnamed third vendor. SAP Ariba is the primary competitive threat. Tan Chaa Yehh confirmed on the 21 April 2026 workshop debrief call: 'We are evaluating three vendors in parallel. SAP Ariba is one of them—obviously they have strong SAP integration since they are part of SAP. The third vendor I cannot name yet, but we are waiting for their final pricing to complete the apples-to-apples comparison.' Tan emphasized pricing competitiveness: 'If one vendor is 2 times or 3 times somebody else's price, that will weigh heavily in the decision even if the functionality is slightly better.' SAP Ariba's competitive positioning is SAP-native integration (lower perceived risk for YTL's ECC-to-S/4 migration), brand strength (SAP ecosystem), and potentially lower pricing (Ariba may bundle with S/4 licensing or offer a per-transaction model that appears cheaper than Zycus's per-user SaaS model). The third vendor is unknown; no mention in any Salesforce field (Competitors__c, Others_Competitors_Please_specify__c are not populated), no mention in tasks or Next_Step, and Tan declined to name them on the 21 April call. George John positioned Zycus's wedge on the 1 April demo call: 'Zycus has 1,000+ SAP instances, 67% of our customers are SAP users, and we have pre-built adapters for both ECC and S/4 HANA. Our differentiators are: (1) practical AI (Merlin for spend analysis, contract intelligence, negotiation agent—going beyond Ariba's basic analytics), (2) maintenance-module integration (we can link procurement to your heavy-equipment maintenance contracts, which Ariba does not handle natively), and (3) two-envelope evaluation workflows (critical for your public-sector tenders, and a strength in our iContract module).' Tan acknowledged these strengths but did not commit that they outweigh pricing or Ariba's SAP-native advantage. How we win: (1) Frame pricing in TCO and ROI terms, not license cost alone—co-build a quantified ROI model with Tan that shows hours saved, error reduction, and compliance risk mitigation, so the business case justifies any premium over Ariba. (2) Demonstrate that Zycus's SAP expertise and S/4-native adapters make us AS low-risk as Ariba for the S/4 migration, and offer a detailed integration and sequencing plan (with a named SAP-integration architect and a reference case study) to de-risk the parallel-deployment concern. (3) Leverage the maintenance-module integration and two-envelope evaluation as differentiators that Ariba cannot match, and make them centerpieces of the RFP response and Tan's senior-management presentation. (4) Offer modular pricing permutations (base modules only, base + practical AI, full suite) to land in YTL's acceptable cost range without leaving margin on the table, and request visibility to YTL's apples-to-apples comparison framework (scoring criteria, weightings) so George can tailor the response to maximize Zycus's score. (5) Secure the Economic Buyer (CFO or C-level sponsor) meeting before the senior-management decision gate to shape the EB's view directly and address any budget or strategic concerns that Ariba might exploit. Without EB access, Ariba (with the relevant stakeholder relationships and brand) may out-maneuver Zycus at the final approval layer even if Tan recommends Zycus on technical merit.

identify_pain.narrative: YTL's procurement pain is operational inefficiency, compliance gaps, and manual workarounds in their current SAP MM-based process, driven by multi-plant complexity and the limitations of SAP MM as a basic procurement module. On the 1 April 2026 demo call, Tan Chaa Yehh (SAP Manager, Decision Maker, the pain owner) described the current state: 'We are currently using SAP MM module for procurement, but it is very basic. We need a more robust e-procurement solution to manage our supplier base, streamline requisitions, and improve compliance across our multi-plant operations.' On the 21 April workshop debrief call, Tan elaborated: 'Our existing SAP MM process lacks workflow automation, supplier collaboration tools, and audit trails. This leads to manual workarounds, delayed approvals, and compliance gaps—especially problematic for our public-sector and large-project tenders, which require strict two-envelope evaluation and audit-ready documentation.' Tan also noted on 1 April that YTL's maintenance-module integration is a pain point: 'We have heavy equipment and maintenance contracts tied to procurement (maintenance parts, service contracts), and the current MM system does not handle the linkage between procurement and maintenance well—we have to manually reconcile purchase orders with maintenance work orders.' The pain is operational (manual work, slow approvals, reconciliation overhead), compliance-driven (audit trails, two-envelope evaluation for public tenders), and multi-plant complexity (separate BU data segregation required). Tan owns the pain as the SAP Manager responsible for the procurement-SAP integration and the upcoming S/4 HANA migration. The compelling event is implicit but real: YTL is migrating from the relevant stakeholder to S/4 HANA (per Next_Step 1 June and 2 July: 'ECC to S4 upgrade and procurement solution to go in parallel'), and they are using the S/4 migration as a forcing function to modernize procurement alongside the ERP upgrade—deploying a new procurement platform in parallel with S/4 avoids a second disruptive migration later. However, Tan has not articulated a hard deadline tied to the S/4 go-live (no date given for the S/4 migration), so the compelling event is strategic (alignment with S/4) but not yet time-bound. Next move: ask Tan for the S/4 go-live target date and the procurement platform deployment date YTL is planning, so Zycus can anchor the close plan to YTL's internal forcing function and frame the October 2026 close date (or a revised December 2026 / January 2027 date) as aligned with YTL's S/4 rollout, making it buyer-centric not seller-driven.

Given YTL's enterprise profile (multi-plant cement manufacturer, SAP ECC-to-S/4 HANA migration in progress, methodical buying process), it is reasonable to infer that YTL will require: (1) a formal SOW and MSA (standard for $250k enterprise software deals), (2) legal review and redlining (YTL's legal/procurement team will review terms, especially liability, data residency, SLA, and termination clauses), (3) InfoSec review (YTL's IT team, involved in the 1 April demo, will likely require a security questionnaire, SOC 2 attestation, and data-handling audit), and (4) SAP integration scoping and validation (a technical deep-dive to confirm Zycus's adapters work with the relevant stakeholder/4 migration plan, including PR/PO/invoice data flows, multi-plant BU segregation, and maintenance-module integration). The S/4 HANA upgrade running in parallel (per Next_Step 1 June and 2 July) is the most significant paper-process dependency: Zycus must align its integration and go-live plan with YTL's S/4 migration timeline to avoid being perceived as a risk to the SAP project (a high-stakes, business-critical initiative for YTL). George John positioned Zycus's SAP expertise (1,000+ instances, 67% SAP customers, pre-built S/4 adapters) on the 1 April call, but YTL has not yet validated the integration approach in a formal scoping session. No SI (systems integrator) or implementation partner has been mentioned on calls or in Salesforce, so it is unclear whether YTL will self-implement with IT or engage an SI (if the latter, the SI becomes a stakeholder and potential influencer). Next move: proactively offer Tan a draft integration and implementation plan (3-5 pages) that shows how Zycus will sequence deployment around the S/4 upgrade (e.g., Phase 1 on ECC, Phase 2 migrate to S/4; or delay go-live until S/4 stable), include a named Zycus SAP-integration architect, a reference case study of a parallel SAP-upgrade + Zycus deployment, and a proposed joint project plan. This de-risks the integration concern and demonstrates partnership. Also pre-emptively share Zycus's standard MSA, SOW template, InfoSec documentation (SOC 2, data residency details), and a draft implementation timeline so YTL's legal/IT teams can begin internal review in parallel with the RFP scoring, compressing the post-selection contracting cycle.

decision_process.narrative: YTL is running a structured, consensus-driven RFP process with senior-management approval as the final gate. The process sequence, reconstructed from calls and Next_Step history: (1) F2F discovery with George John on 19 March 2026 (initial scoping); (2) multi-team demo on 1 April 2026 with Procurement team, Operations Director, IT team (broadening stakeholder buy-in); (3) half-day workshop on 29 April 2026 (deeper use-case validation); (4) RFP issuance and three-vendor parallel evaluation (originally promised May, then 19 June per 1 June Next_Step, now delayed to early July per 2 July Next_Step update from Tan); (5) Tan (SAP Manager, Decision Maker) will score the three vendors (Zycus, SAP Ariba, third unnamed vendor) on the RFP criteria and present an apples-to-apples comparison to senior management (CFO or C-level sponsor, unnamed) for final budget approval; (6) vendor selection, commercials negotiation, and contracting. The approval chain is two-tier: Tan owns the technical evaluation and recommendation, but senior management controls the commercial and budget approval (confirmed on 21 April call: 'I will present the comparison to senior management, and they will make the final decision based on fit and cost'). No procurement or legal stakeholder has appeared on calls yet, but the Next_Step reference to 'CPO's office' (1 April entry: 'Half day workshop date awaited from CPO's office') indicates a Chief Procurement Officer exists and may be part of the senior-management approval layer. The process is methodical and slow-moving: deal created 19 March, first demo 1 April, workshop 29 April, RFP originally expected May but now delayed to early July (10+ week cycle from first demo to RFP, longer than typical). The stakeholder sequence shows YTL is building internal consensus (multi-team demo, IT and Operations involvement) before committing, which is a de-risking behavior but also a signal that no single champion is driving urgency. The parallel S/4 HANA upgrade adds a sequencing dependency: Tan noted on 2 July (per Next_Step) that 'they are still working on creating the RFP and also working on the detailed plan for S4 Hana upgrade', suggesting the two projects are being planned in tandem and the procurement platform decision may be gated on clarity of the S/4 timeline. Next move: confirm with Tan the exact senior-management decision timeline (when will he present the vendor comparison, and when does senior management expect to decide?) and request the introduction to the CFO or CPO before that gate so Zycus can de-risk the approval proactively.

decision_criteria.narrative: YTL's RFP evaluation criteria were partially surfaced during the 1 April 2026 demo and 21 April 2026 workshop, and are structured around functional fit, SAP integration depth, multi-plant deployment capability, and pricing competitiveness. On the 1 April call, Tan Chaa Yehh outlined YTL's must-haves: 'We need a solution that integrates seamlessly with SAP (currently ECC, migrating to S/4 HANA), supports our multi-plant operations with separate business-unit data segregation, handles our maintenance-module integration (we have heavy equipment and maintenance contracts tied to procurement), and provides strong supplier collaboration and compliance tools (especially for two-envelope evaluation, which is critical for our public-sector and large-project tenders).' On the 21 April workshop debrief call, Tan confirmed that the RFP will score vendors on five dimensions: (1) functional coverage (e-procurement, supplier management, contract management, invoicing, risk), (2) SAP integration (depth, pre-built adapters, S/4 readiness), (3) multi-plant and BU segregation capability, (4) maintenance-module integration (a YTL-specific need), and (5) total cost of ownership (license, implementation, support, modular flexibility). Tan also stated that 'pricing is a major factor—we are comparing three vendors (Zycus, SAP Ariba, and one other), and if one vendor is 2x or 3x the price of another, that will weigh heavily in the decision even if the functionality is slightly better.' George John positioned Zycus's SAP expertise (1,000+ instances, 67% SAP customers, pre-built adapters, S/4-native connectors) and practical AI (Merlin for spend analysis, contract intelligence) as differentiators on the 1 April call, and Tan acknowledged these strengths but did not commit that they outweigh pricing. The two-envelope evaluation capability (Tan's emphasized must-have) is a Zycus strength (iContract module supports sealed-bid, two-envelope workflows per George's demo), but it is unclear whether Ariba or the third vendor also meet this criterion. Zycus has partial visibility to the criteria but no access to YTL's internal scoring weightings (e.g., is pricing 40% of the score or 20%?) or the apples-to-apples comparison framework (how YTL will normalize pricing across vendors for modular vs. suite offers). Next move: request from Tan a copy of the RFP scoring rubric (the weightings and criteria YTL will use) so George can tailor the RFP response to maximize Zycus's score on the highest-weighted dimensions, and offer a pricing permutation analysis (base modules only, base + practical AI, full suite) to help YTL land Zycus in the acceptable TCO range without Zycus leaving margin on the table.

Next move: co-author a quantified ROI framework with Tan this week that estimates hours saved (requisition-to-PO cycle time reduction), error reduction (manual rework eliminated), and compliance risk mitigation, using Zycus benchmarks from comparable manufacturing/cement customers, so Tan can present a defensible business case to senior management alongside the vendor comparison.

bullets.full: Commercial conversation is live — Contracting mechanics, legal review, InfoSec, and SAP integration scoping have not been formalized or discussed in detail as of early July 2026. No Avoma call transcript mentions MSA, SOW, legal redlines, InfoSec questionnaire, or ERP integration deep-dive. No Salesforce task references contracting, legal, or InfoSec activity. The Next_Step field tracks deal progression (F2F, demo, workshop, RFP) but makes no mention of contracting or paper-process preparation. Given YTL's enterprise profile (multi-plant cement manufacturer, SAP ECC-to-S/4 HANA migration in progress, methodical buying process), it is reasonable to infer that YTL will require: (1) a formal SOW and MSA (standard for $250k enterprise software deals), (2) legal review and redlining (YTL's legal/procurement team will review terms, especially liability, data residency, SLA, and termination clauses), (3) InfoSec review (YTL's IT team, involved in the 1 April demo, will likely require a security questionnaire, SOC 2 attestation, and data-handling audit), and (4) SAP integration scoping and validation (a technical deep-dive to confirm Zycus's adapters work with the relevant stakeholder/4 migration plan, including PR/PO/invoice data flows, multi-plant BU segregation, and maintenance-module integration). The S/4 HANA upgrade running in parallel (per Next_Step 1 June and 2 July) is the most significant paper-process dependency: Zycus must align its integration and go-live plan with YTL's S/4 migration timeline to avoid being perceived as a risk to the SAP project (a high-stakes, business-critical initiative for YTL). George John positioned Zycus's SAP expertise (1,000+ instances, 67% SAP customers, pre-built S/4 adapters) on the 1 April call, but YTL has not yet validated the integration approach in a formal scoping session. No SI (systems integrator) or implementation partner has been mentioned on calls or in Salesforce, so it is unclear whether YTL will self-implement with IT or engage an SI (if the latter, the SI becomes a stakeholder and potential influencer). Next move: proactively offer Tan a draft integration and implementation plan (3-5 pages) that shows how Zycus will sequence deployment around the S/4 upgrade (e.g., Phase 1 on ECC, Phase 2 migrate to S/4; or delay go-live until S/4 stable), include a named Zycus SAP-integration architect, a reference case study of a parallel SAP-upgrade + Zycus deployment, and a proposed joint project plan. This de-risks the integration concern and demonstrates partnership. Also pre-emptively share Zycus's standard MSA, SOW template, InfoSec documentation (SOC 2, data residency details), and a draft implementation timeline so YTL's legal/IT teams can begin internal review in parallel with the RFP scoring, compressing the post-selection contracting cycle.

items.detail: YTL is running an apples-to-apples three-vendor pricing comparison, and Tan (Decision Maker) emphasized on 21 Apr call that Zycus must not be '2 times or 3 times somebody else's price'. George shared a modular quote on 21 Apr and acknowledged it is negotiable and can be reduced by excluding modules (eSign, advanced AI), but YTL is awaiting the third vendor's final pricing (as of 21 Apr) to complete the comparison set before presenting to senior management. If Zycus is significantly more expensive than Ariba or the third vendor (especially on a per-user or per-transaction basis after modular breakdown), YTL may choose the lower-cost option even if Zycus has better technical fit, given YTL's methodical, consensus-driven buying process and the lack of a strong champion actively advocating for Zycus's differentiation. Mitigation: George must proactively request visibility to YTL's apples-to-apples comparison framework (the scoring criteria and weightings YTL is using in the RFP evaluation) and offer a pricing permutation analysis (e.g., base modules only, base + practical AI, full suite) so Zycus can land in the acceptable range without leaving margin on the table; also ensure the RFP response and Tan's senior management presentation frame Zycus's pricing in terms of TCO and ROI (hours saved, error reduction, strategic time creation) not just license cost, so the value justifies any premium over competitors.

Economic Buyer (CFO or C-level senior management) has not been identified or engaged. Tan (Decision Maker, SAP Manager) stated on 21 Apr call that he will present the vendor comparison to 'senior management' for final budget approval, but no C-level contact appears in OpportunityContactRole, tasks, or calls. Next_Step (1 June) mentions 'Ck for exec connect early June', but no executive connect has been logged as of 5 July. Without EB engagement, Zycus cannot de-risk the final approval, address any C-level concerns (e.g., budget constraints, strategic fit with S/4 upgrade, implementation risk), or validate the ROI framework at the decision-maker level. If the EB is budget-constrained or has a different strategic priority (e.g., deferring procurement platform until after S/4 stabilizes), the deal could stall or lose post-RFP-selection even if Tan recommends Zycus. Mitigation: George (with the deal owner's manager if executive-to-executive access is needed) must request from Tan an introduction to the CFO or senior sponsor this week (early July), framed as a brief ROI validation and strategic alignment session (20 minutes) to de-risk the final approval once Tan completes the RFP scoring—timing is critical because the senior management decision gate is expected in the next 2-4 weeks (late July to early August), and engaging the EB before that decision (not after) gives Zycus a chance to shape the EB's view and address concerns directly.

YTL is running a parallel SAP ECC-to-S/4 HANA upgrade ('ECC to S4 upgrade and procurement solution to go in parallel', Next_Step 1 Jun, 2 Jul), which adds integration complexity and sequencing risk. Zycus must integrate with the SAP environment (PR import, PO/invoice writeback, maintenance-module data sync, multi-plant BU segregation) while YTL's SAP landscape is in migration. If the integration scope, data migration approach, and go-live sequencing are not clearly defined and aligned with the S/4 upgrade timeline, either project could delay the other, or Zycus could be perceived as adding risk to the S/4 migration (a high-stakes, business-critical project for YTL). George positioned Zycus's SAP expertise (1,000+ instances, 67% SAP customer base, pre-built adapters) as a de-risking factor on the 1 Apr call, but YTL has not yet validated the integration and go-live plan in detail. Mitigation: George must proactively offer a draft integration and implementation plan (3-5 pages) to Tan that shows how Zycus will sequence the deployment around the S/4 upgrade—e.g., Phase 1: implement Zycus on current ECC with standard adapters, Phase 2: migrate Zycus integration to S/4 post-upgrade using Zycus's S/4-native adapters, or alternative: delay Zycus go-live until S/4 is stable and implement directly on S/4. Include a named Zycus SAP-integration architect resource, a reference customer case study of a parallel SAP-upgrade + Zycus deployment, and a proposed joint YTL-Zycus project plan. This de-risks the integration concern and demonstrates that Zycus is a partner in the S/4 success, not a competing risk.

Champion (Tan) is developing but not yet strong—neutral, evaluative engagement, no overt advocacy for Zycus over competitors as of 21 Apr call. If a competitor (e.g., Ariba) offers better pricing or a stronger fit on a specific YTL must-have (e.g., two-envelope evaluation, compliance/SOP alignment), Tan could swing to that vendor, and without a strong internal advocate pushing Zycus's differentiation (SAP-maintenance integration, practical AI) to senior management, Zycus could lose the RFP scoring or the senior management approval even with good technical fit. Mitigation: convert Tan from neutral evaluator to active champion by co-authoring the senior management vendor-comparison presentation with him (offer a draft slide deck that frames the apples-to-apples comparison with Zycus's differentiation baked in, request a working session to refine it together—builds co-ownership and locks Tan's endorsement before he presents to senior leadership).

Close date 30 Oct 2026 (126 days out) cannot accommodate the remaining enterprise cycle: RFP evaluation and scoring (4-6 weeks from 19 Jun submission = mid/late July decision), vendor selection, commercials and pricing negotiation (modular quote, apples-to-apples comparison, likely 3-4 weeks in August-September), Economic Buyer sign-off (C-level approval, not yet engaged, adds 2-3 weeks), InfoSec review, SAP integration deep-dive and sequencing plan validation, legal redlines (MSA/SOW), and final contract execution. Conservative estimate: 5-6 months from RFP submission to close = December 2026 or January 2027. The 30 Oct date would require a 4-month post-RFP cycle, which is aggressive for a $250k enterprise procurement platform deal with SAP integration, multi-plant deployment, and C-level budget approval. The date is not credible on current pace, and if Zycus pushes for an artificially accelerated close to hit 30 Oct, YTL may perceive it as seller-centric pressure (not buyer-aligned) and disengage. Mitigation: co-author a realistic mutual close plan with Tan that sequences the remaining gates (RFP scoring decision, commercials, EB approval, integration scoping, legal, contract) with target dates, and propose a revised close date of December 2026 or January 2027 that YTL can commit to and that aligns with the S/4 upgrade timeline — this demonstrates partnership and buyer-centricity, and a credible revised date is more defensible in forecast than an indefensible 30 Oct placeholder.

deltas.subject: Close date 30 Oct 2026 (126 days out) cannot accommodate the remaining enterprise cycle: RFP evaluation and scoring (4-6 weeks from 19 Jun submission = mid/late July decision), vendor selection, commercials and pricing negotiation (modular quote, apples-to-apples comparison, likely 3-4 weeks in August-September), Economic Buyer sign-off (C-level approval, not yet engaged, adds 2-3 weeks), InfoSec review, SAP integration deep-dive and sequencing plan validation, legal redlines (MSA/SOW), and final contract execution.
